contains_no_content_of_requested_type.description: Set to true if the input text contains no valid recipe entries after applying the validity gate; otherwise false. Three whole-passage classes MUST yield true with an empty entries array even though their items look procedural: (1) medical remedy sections — ailment-headed receipt collections, dose-and-indication framed preparations, physician-attributed cures — even when the items are ingestible ales, wines, or waters; (2) menus, bills of fare, and past-tense banquet or service relations (accounts of what was served at named events, course lists, scalcheria) — even when individual items carry embedded preparation detail; (3) craft or technical secrets and non-consumable production (dyes, metallurgy, cosmetics, inks, topical salves and plasters, household cleaning).

entries.description: List of historical recipe entries extracted from the text; empty array if none. An entry is a preparation of a CONSUMABLE foodstuff or beverage with enough procedural text present in the input to execute it. VALIDITY GATE — never extract: (a) therapeutic preparations framed by dose-and-indication (ailment heading, a dose tied to a complaint, or physician attribution); an ordinary consumable (cordial, liqueur, flavored wine or spirit) IS an entry even inside a medical section — judge framing per item, not per chapter; (b) menu/bill-of-fare items and past-tense banquet or service accounts, even when an item embeds preparation detail; (c) non-consumables (craft secrets, dyes, cosmetics, topical salves/plasters/ointments, metallurgy, household cleaning). SEGMENTATION — one entry per individual recipe: each TITLED variant in a variant run is its own entry, including terse 'make like the previous' variants that name a distinct principal ingredient or subject (e.g. '... ganz nach Nr. 4', 'bereidt men evenals de vorige', 'ARE made with lemons as these'), with fields inherited from the anchor where textually supported; never merge a run of sub-titled variants under a group heading into a single entry (a group heading carrying its own procedural text is an additional entry); when several title-only variants share ONE closing instruction block, create one entry per variant and attach the shared block to each; untitled but self-contained preparations (e.g. a run of headerless sauces, each for a different dish) are entries with title_original null; base preparations and technique recipes (stocks, doughs, sugar-boiling stages, clarification) are entries; do NOT split a single continuous preparation or a numbered yield/statistics report into multiple entries. NOT entries: pure cross-reference pointer stubs whose only body redirects elsewhere ('See page N.', 'Zie n°. 98'); bare titles with no method text; a chunk-opening orphan fragment that continues a recipe begun before this input (headerless text starting mid-sentence). A recipe truncated at the END of the input IS extracted with the text available in the input. Work through the ENTIRE input before finishing — long inputs routinely contain 15-25 entries; do not stop mid-list.

recipe_text_original.description: Complete recipe text verbatim in its original language (preserve spelling, diacritics, and formatting), without page numbers or other formatting elements. Reproduce the source exactly, INCLUDING OCR corruption and archaic spellings — never repair, normalize, or modernize in this field (all repairs belong in recipe_text_modern_english). For a titled variant governed by a shared closing instruction block, append that block verbatim after the variant's own title/line.

recipe_text_modern_english.description: Modern English translation of the complete recipe text. If the recipe is already in English, produce a fully modernized English rendering.

title_original.description: Recipe title exactly as it appears in the source (preserve spelling and diacritics). Use null if no title is given.

title_modern_english.description: Modern English translation of the original recipe title. If the title is already in English, produce a fully modernized English rendering.

recipe_type.description: Primary category based on the recipe's main purpose. 'Brewing/Winemaking' and 'Distillation' for production processes; 'Beverage' for drink-serving preparations. 'Dairy Production' for cheesemaking, butter churning, etc. 'Vinegar/Oil/Essence' for vinegar, oil pressing, rosewater, herb essences. 'Household Recipe' only for the rare non-food preparation that nonetheless passes the validity gate — medicinal salves, plasters, and remedies are NOT entries of any type and must not be extracted at all. 'Preparation Technique' for base preparations (stocks, pastry doughs). 'Other' if identifiable but not listed; 'Unknown' if classification is impossible.

ingredients.description: Ingredients with original names, translations, and quantities. Empty array if no ingredients can be reliably separated.

name_original.description: Ingredient name as it appears in source (preserve spelling and diacritics).

name_modern_english.description: Modern English translation of the ingredient. If already in English, produce a fully modernized English rendering.

quantity_original.description: Quantity as recorded in source. Use null if not specified.

origin_explicitly_stated.description: If the recipe text explicitly states an origin (e.g., 'Spanish oil'), record verbatim. Null if none.

ingredient_luxury_signal_rating_1_7.description: TIME-INVARIANT luxury signal, anchored to ONE fixed reference frame: the ingredient's relative cost and prestige in European markets around the year 1650. Rate every work on this single pan-European c. 1650 frame — do NOT adjust for the work's own date, and do NOT adjust for its region; an ingredient receives the same score in a 1550 Italian work and an 1880 German one. 1 = staple cheap and ubiquitous across Europe c. 1650 (bread grain, onions, cabbage, local vegetables, lard); 2 = common local produce; 3 = modest market goods; 4 = moderately valued goods c. 1650 (e.g. common spices such as pepper, refined sugar in ordinary use); 5 = prosperous-household ingredient c. 1650 (finer spices, imported dried fruits, almonds in quantity); 6 = elite luxury c. 1650 (saffron, fine sugar confectionery stock, costly imported delicacies); 7 = the highest-cost rarities c. 1650 (exotic imported delicacies, extreme rarities, ambergris-grade luxuries). Borderline convention: when an ingredient sits between two bands, score the lower band unless the item is unambiguously a prestige good in the c. 1650 frame. Use null only if the ingredient cannot be identified well enough to place it.

ingredient_trade_distance_rating_1_7.description: TIME-INVARIANT geographic-origin class of the ingredient. Rate the ingredient's ORIGIN CLASS on a FIXED scale, identical across the whole corpus; do NOT adjust for the work's period (once-exotic goods do NOT descend the scale as trade matures) and do NOT adjust for local availability or price. 1 = ubiquitous local European produce (onions, cabbage, lard, local grain, domestic vegetables, game); 2 = nearby regional European trade (regional grains, dairy, freshwater and coastal fish, Baltic herring); 3 = broader intra-European trade (olive oil carried north, citrus, rice, wine moved over distance, Mediterranean nuts); 4 = Mediterranean and Levantine imports (dried fruits of the Levant — currants, raisins, dates, figs — almonds, capers); 5 = Atlantic-fringe and early-colonial staples (cane sugar, Madeira/Canaries produce, Atlantic-island imports); 6 = established trans-oceanic commodities (pepper, cinnamon, nutmeg, cloves, ginger, mace, and other established East-Indies or New-World trade goods); 7 = far trans-oceanic or rare-route rarities (exotic New World or East Asian rarities reaching Europe only by long or unusual routes). Score the origin class the ingredient belongs to, not how common it had become by the work's date.

ingredient_novelty_rating_1_7.description: TIME-INVARIANT novelty, anchored PURELY to the ingredient's date of WIDESPREAD EUROPEAN ADOPTION — a per-ingredient constant, the same in every work regardless of the work's own date. Do NOT adjust for whether the ingredient would 'still have seemed novel' when this particular work was written; a New World crop scores the same in a 1600 source and an 1850 source. 1 = antiquity or early-medieval staple known in Europe for a millennium or more (wheat, pork, onions, honey, wine, cabbage); 2 = ingredient established in Europe before c. 1400 by long-distance trade or medieval diffusion (rice, buckwheat, Levantine spices such as pepper and cinnamon); 3 = late-medieval to early-16th-century groceries widespread before c. 1500 (refined cane sugar, distilled spirits); 4 = 16th-century New World or exotic arrivals adopted in Europe during the 1500s (turkey, common beans, maize where used); 5 = 17th-century adoptions that became widespread across Europe (coffee, tea, chocolate/cocoa, tomato and chocolate in cookery); 6 = 18th-century adoptions reaching general European use (the potato as a widespread staple, and comparable 1700s introductions); 7 = post-1800 adoptions (19th-century novelties and newly introduced ingredients). Base the score solely on the fixed adoption date; refined sugar rates 3, never 1.

cooking_methods.description: Cooking techniques used in this recipe with original terms and translations. Return empty array if none are mentioned.

method_original.description: Cooking method as it appears in source (preserve spelling).

method_modern_english.description: Modern English translation. If already in English, produce a fully modernized English rendering.

method_complexity_rating_1_7.description: Rate the skill and labor intensity required to execute this technique in a period kitchen. 1 = simple common household technique requiring minimal training (boiling, basic roasting, salting); 4 = moderately skilled procedure requiring attention and practice (making a roux, clarifying stock, careful frying with temperature judgment); 7 = highly technical method requiring professional training or complex equipment (spun-sugar work, multi-stage reduction with liaison, elaborate laminated pastry). Use null if insufficient evidence.

utensils_equipment.description: Cooking tools and equipment mentioned in the recipe. Return empty array if none are mentioned.

utensil_original.description: Utensil as it appears in source (preserve spelling).

utensil_modern_english.description: Modern English translation. If already in English, produce a fully modernized rendering.

utensil_specialization_rating_1_7.description: Rate how specialized and costly this piece of equipment was for its period. 1 = universal household item found in virtually any kitchen (earthen pot, knife, wooden spoon); 4 = moderately specialized, requiring a degree of investment (chafing dish, strainer, pastry board); 7 = highly specialized equipment found only in affluent or commercial kitchens (clockwork spit, copper confectionary mold, distillation apparatus).

utensil_modernity_rating_1_7.description: Rate this piece of equipment on the culinary-historical spectrum, using the same reference frame as the recipe-level modernity_rating_1_7. 1 = equipment typical only of pre-modern kitchens (cauldron, spit, stone mortar); 4 = post-La Varenne era kitchen (c.1651+); 7 = equipment characteristic of the high classical professional kitchen (Escoffier era).

timing_yield.description: Yield, preparation time, and cooking time as stated in the source. Historical recipes rarely specify these; use null for unstated fields.

yield_original.description: Yield as stated verbatim in the source (e.g., 'pour six personnes', 'for a large table'). Use null if not specified.

preparation_time_original.description: Preparation time as stated verbatim in the source. Use null if not specified.

cooking_time_original.description: Cooking time as stated verbatim in the source. Use null if not specified.

ingredient_categories.description: Boolean flags for ingredient categories relevant to historical culinary analysis.

contains_meat.description: True if recipe contains red meat from mammals (beef, pork, lamb, veal, venison, rabbit, etc.). False otherwise. Exclude poultry and fish.

contains_poultry.description: True if recipe contains domestic or wild birds (chicken, duck, goose, turkey, pigeon, pheasant, etc.). False otherwise.

contains_fish_seafood.description: True if recipe contains any fish, shellfish, crustaceans, or other aquatic creatures. False otherwise.

contains_dairy.description: True if recipe contains milk, cream, cheese, or other dairy products. False otherwise. Note: butter has its own separate flag.

contains_eggs.description: True if the recipe contains eggs. False otherwise.

contains_butter.description: True if the recipe specifically contains butter. False otherwise.

contains_olive_oil.description: True if the recipe specifically contains olive oil. False otherwise.

contains_lard_animal_fat.description: True if the recipe contains lard or other animal fats that are not butter. False otherwise.

contains_alcohol.description: True if the recipe contains alcoholic beverages (wine, beer, spirits, etc.). False otherwise.

contains_refined_sugar.description: True if the recipe contains refined sugar. False otherwise.

contains_honey.description: True if the recipe contains honey. False otherwise.

contains_other_sweeteners.description: True if recipe contains sweeteners other than honey or refined sugar (molasses, maple, fruit syrups, dried fruits as sweeteners). False otherwise.

contains_foreign_spices.description: True if recipe contains spices imported to Europe via long-distance trade (pepper, cinnamon, cloves, nutmeg, mace, ginger, saffron, cardamom, etc.). False otherwise. Exclude local herbs.

contains_luxury_ingredients.description: True if recipe contains ingredients that signaled high status in the historical period (truffles, caviar, ortolans, elaborate pastry work, expensive cuts, imported delicacies). False otherwise.

culinary_style.description: Culinary-historical style classification based on technique, ingredient use, and structural features.

modernity_rating_1_7.description: Position on the culinary-historical spectrum. 1 = late-medieval style; 4 = transitional; 7 = mature 18th/19th-c. haute cuisine. Use null if ambiguous or absent. Calibration: anchor on the work's date and style regime rather than defaulting to the middle of the scale — a routine recipe in a 19th-century professional or bourgeois cookbook normally sits at 5-6, a 16th-17th-century recipe with archaic markers at 2-3; reserve 4 for genuinely transitional evidence.

innovation_markers_observed.description: Innovation markers detected in the text. Empty if none.

enum: Egg or cream liaison for finishing
Mise en place or advance prep mentioned

archaism_markers_observed.description: Markers suggesting older culinary style. Empty if none.

enum: Heavy mixed spicing (cinnamon + pepper + ginger etc.)
Whole roast or spit-based method dominant

intertextuality.description: Textual signals of attribution, borrowing, cross-referencing, and rhetorical claims about tradition, authenticity, or national identity.

explicit_source_attribution.description: If the text explicitly names a source/authority, record verbatim. Null if none.

explicit_foreign_style_reference.description: If the text explicitly names a foreign style or national manner, record verbatim. Null if none.

self_positioning_temporal.description: How the recipe text positions itself relative to culinary tradition and innovation.

tradition_claim_present.description: True if the recipe text claims tradition/old custom. False if not explicitly present.

authenticity_claim_present.description: True if the recipe text claims authenticity/genuineness/originality. False if not explicitly present.

national_identity_claim_present.description: True if the recipe text invokes national identity explicitly. False if not explicitly present.

anti_foreign_sentiment_present.description: True if the recipe text disparages foreign cuisine or asserts native superiority. False if not explicitly present.

geographic_signals.description: Geographic references explicitly present in the recipe text.

place_references.description: Places explicitly named in recipe text. Empty if none.

place_name_original.description: Place name exactly as in the text.

economic_signals.description: Economic signals, occasion context, and luxury/prestige indicators.

economic_framing_detected.description: Types of economic framing detected in the recipe text. Empty array if none.

luxury_intensity_rating_1_7.description: TIME-INVARIANT overall luxury/prestige signaling of the whole recipe, anchored to ONE fixed reference frame: the relative cost and prestige of its ingredients, technique, and presentation in European markets around the year 1650. Rate every work on this single pan-European c. 1650 frame — do NOT adjust for the work's own date, and do NOT adjust for its region. 1 = no luxury signaling (subsistence fare cheap everywhere c. 1650); 4 = moderate status markers c. 1650; 7 = maximal prestige display (elite or courtly extravagance in the c. 1650 frame). Borderline convention: an ordinary recipe without explicit status markers sits at 2-3, and the mere presence of sugar, common spices, or butter does not by itself raise it above 3-4; reserve 5 for genuine prosperous-household display and 6-7 for elite or courtly extravagance, all judged against c. 1650. Use null if ambiguous.

occasion_type.description: Occasion signals detected. Empty array if none.

religious_signals.description: Religious, liturgical calendar, and moral-virtue signals.

fasting_context_indicated.description: True if marked for fasting days/Lent/vigils. False if not explicitly indicated.

meat_day_context_indicated.description: True if marked for meat days or explicitly non-fasting. False if not explicitly indicated.

confessional_hint.description: Only classify if text contains explicit markers.

moral_virtue_framing_present.description: True if recipe framing includes moral language (virtue of frugality, temperance, avoiding excess). False if not explicitly indicated.
